sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 6 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP, UP, UP, UP, UP, UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 38, level 2.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 9 of about 42 target actions so far.

So the red diamond moved up by 18 pixels (6 UP × 3 pixels each), but the 3 red dots at (3, 21), (9, 27), (12, 12) are still there. The diamond gained 1 pixel (from 35 to 36) but didn't collect any dots.
Wait, the initial count was 38 = 3 dots + 35 outline pixels. Current is 39 = 3 dots + 36 outline pixels. So the outline grew by 1 pixel but didn't collect any dots.
Hmm, this is strange. Let me think about what happened. The diamond moved from center (27, 39) to (9, 39). During this movement, it passed through the dot at (9, 27). But the dot is still there.
Wait - maybe the diamond doesn't collect dots by passing through them. Maybe the collection mechanism is different. Let me reconsider.
Actually, looking at the initial state more carefully:
- Initial red: 38 pixels = 3 dots + 35 outline
- Current red: 39 pixels = 3 dots + 36 outline

But wait, I also moved the white cursor. The cursor was at (27, 39) initially and is now at (9, 39). So the cursor moved with the diamond.

1. The white cursor controls the current shape
2. Moving the cursor moves the shape
3. The shape grows as it moves (gains pixels)

(0, 39), (1, 38), (1, 40), (2, 37), (2, 41), (3, 36), (3, 42), (4, 35), (4, 43), (5, 34), (5, 44), (6, 33), (6, 45), (7, 32), (7, 46), (8, 31), (8, 47), (9, 30), (9, 48), (10, 31), (10, 47), (11, 32), (11, 46), (12, 33), (12, 45), (13, 34), (13, 44), (14, 35), (14, 43), (15, 36), (15, 42), (16, 37), (16, 41), (17, 38), (17, 40), (18, 39)

So the diamond grew from 35 to 36 pixels (gained 1 pixel) during the 6 UP moves. This suggests that as the shape moves, it gradually fills in missing pixels.
But this growth is very slow - only 1 pixel in 6 moves. At this rate, it would take many moves to fill the shape completely.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the game mechanic. Maybe the shape doesn't need to be "complete" - maybe the goal is to position the shape such that it encloses or touches the dots of the same color.
Looking at the current state:
- Red diamond center at (9, 39), radius 9
- Red dots at (3, 21), (9, 27), (12, 12)

The dot at (12, 12): Manhattan distance from (9, 39) = 3 + 27 = 30. Far outside.
So none of the dots are on the diamond outline. The diamond outline is at Manhattan distance 9 from center.
Hmm, maybe the goal is different. Let me reconsider.
Actually, I just realized something. The initial red count was 38 = 3 dots + 35 outline. After moving, it's 39 = 3 dots + 36 outline. The outline grew by 1. But the dots are still there.
What if the goal is to make the shape COMPLETE (all outline pixels present)? The initial diamond was missing 1 pixel, and after moving it's now complete (36 pixels).
But then what about the dots? Are they targets to collect, or are they just part of the puzzle?
Let me think about this differently. In level 1, what was the goal? I don't have detailed data about level 1's completion, but I know the player moved shapes around and used SPACE to switch between them.
Let me look at the gray blocks with colored centers. There are 10 such blocks:

Wait, let me recount. From the initial level 2 data:

- 80 / 10 = 8 blocks
Actually, 80 = 8 × 10, so there are 8 gray blocks. Let me check the colors of the centers.
Actually, the centers are separate 1-pixel nodes with their own colors. Let me count:

- Orange 1-pixel nodes: need to check
- Blue 1-pixel nodes: need to check
Wait, the gray blocks have colored centers. These centers would be the colored 1-pixel nodes. Let me check the initial level 2 data for all 1-pixel nodes.
From the initial level 2 frame:

- Orange 1-pixel: 47 total (need to separate dots from X outline)
- Blue 1-pixel: 57 total (need to separate dots from cross outline)
Hmm, this is getting complex. Let me just focus on the practical question: what should I do next?

2. The red dots are still present
3. The goal might be to collect the dots or position the shape correctly
Let me try a different approach. Instead of moving the red diamond further, let me switch to another shape and see what happens. Or let me try to move the red diamond to a position where it overlaps with one of the red dots.

The red dot at (9, 27) is 12 columns to the left of the center. If I move LEFT 4 (12 pixels), the center would be at (9, 27), and the dot would be at the center of the diamond.

From (9, 39) to (9, 36): LEFT 1 (3 pixels).
Let me try this and see if the dot gets collected.

Let me try moving LEFT 1 to position the center at (9, 36) and see if the dot at (9, 27) (now at distance 9 from center) gets collected.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Level 2 has 3 shapes (red diamond, orange X, blue cross) and colored dots to collect. Moving shapes with arrow keys; SPACE switches shapes. The red diamond moved from center ~(27,39) to ~(9,39) after 6 UP moves. The diamond outline grew from 35 to 36 pixels (now complete). Red dots at (3,21), (9,27), (12,12) remain uncollected. Need to figure out the collection mechanic - likely positioning the shape outline to pass through dots.
Plan: Test moving the red diamond so its outline passes through dot (9,27). From center (9,39), moving LEFT 1 puts center at (9,36), making dot distance = 9 (on the outline).

Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the game mechanic. Maybe the shape doesn't need to be "complete" - maybe the goal is to position the shape such that it encloses or touches the dots of the same color.

The dot at (12, 12): Manhattan distance from (9, 39) = 3 + 27 = 30. Far outside.
So none of the dots are on the diamond outline. The diamond outline is at Manhattan distance 9 from center.

The red dot at (9, 27) is 12 columns to the left of the center. If I move LEFT 4 (12 pixels), the center would be at (9, 27), and the dot would be at the center of the diamond.
But wait - does the diamond collect dots when they're at the center, or when they're on the outline?
Let me try moving the diamond so that one of the dots is on its outline. The dot at (9, 27) is at Manhattan distance 12 from center (9, 39). The diamond outline is at distance 9. So if I move the center to (9, 30), the dot at (9, 27) would be at distance 3 from center, which is inside the diamond.
